did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used artificial intelligence, specifically deep learning (Convolutional Neural Networks such as AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogleNet, ResNet, and DenseNet) and conventional computer vision methods (hand-crafted features like SIFT, LBP, HOG combined with SVM), to perform binary classification for the preliminary screening of acral melanoma from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the collection of a new dataset (AMPSD with 6,066 images), the implementation of various computer vision and deep learning algorithms (AlexNet, VGGNet, etc.), and the reporting of new performance results (accuracy and AUROC) for automatic acral melanoma screening. It is published in the IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine (BIBM).

title: A Benchmark for Automatic Acral Melanoma Preliminary Screening
journal: 2018 IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine (BIBM)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automatic preliminary screening for acral melanoma, a type of skin cancer under nails, by introducing the Acral Melanoma Preliminary Screening Dataset (AMPSD). The dataset contains 6,066 dermoscopic images annotated by senior dermatologists to distinguish subungual hematomas from other malignant symptoms. The authors evaluated the task using both conventional computer vision methods with hand-crafted features and state-of-the-art deep learning Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Performance was assessed using accuracy and Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) metrics on a held-out test set. The results demonstrated that CNNs significantly outperformed conventional methods, with VGGNet achieving the best performance of 0.9276 accuracy and 0.9666 AUROC. The paper concludes that while CNNs are effective, further improvements are needed, and the released dataset aims to facilitate future research in this area.

lesion_types_studied.role: Target class (Negative class in the binary classification task)
lesion_types_studied.details: The most common benign symptom in the AMPSD dataset, comprising 1,950 images. Labeled as negative examples (y=0) for the preliminary screening task.

paper_terms: other acral melanoma symptoms of malignant tendency
lesion_types_studied.role: Target class (Positive class in the binary classification task)
lesion_types_studied.details: Symptoms indicating malignant tendency under nails, comprising 4,116 images. Labeled as positive examples (y=1) for the preliminary screening task. Includes conditions requiring medical treatment.

all_task_types.target: Binary label indicating subungual hematomas (benign) vs. other acral melanoma symptoms of malignant tendency
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly defines the task as 'Preliminary screening of acral melanoma is a binary classification task' where the output is a binary label y ∈ {0, 1}. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant symptoms) using dermoscopic images. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and AUROC.

datasets_used.name: Acral Melanoma Preliminary Screening Dataset (AMPSD)
datasets_used.description: A large dataset of dermoscopic images collected during clinical treatment at Peking University First Hospital over more than one year. The dataset was curated by carefully removing duplicate and similar images to maintain diversity. It is annotated by two chief physicians with more than 10 years of clinical experience in dermatology.
classes_used: Other acral melanoma symptoms of malignant tendency (positive examples)

use.training: Used to train and evaluate conventional computer vision methods (hand-crafted features + SVM) and deep learning CNNs (AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogleNet, ResNet, DenseNet). The training set comprised 4,021 images.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the classification algorithms. The test set comprised 2,045 images with approximately equal category ratio to the training set.
use.other: Released as a benchmark dataset to encourage further research on acral melanoma recognition.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification algorithms on the test set.
To evaluate classifier performance, particularly to address the issue of imbalanced data where accuracy alone is insufficient.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and AUROC metrics on a held-out test set without statistical significance testing.

AMPSD (Acral Melanoma Preliminary Screen.notes: VGGNet+ft achieved the best performance with 0.9276 accuracy and 0.9666 AUROC. Conventional methods performed poorly (accuracy 0.61-0.71), while all CNNs reached at least 0.90 accuracy and 0.95 AUROC. Pre-trained networks (ft) generally outperformed non-pre-trained ones by about 3% in accuracy. ResNet and DenseNet showed signs of overfitting compared to VGGNet.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogleNet, ResNet, and DenseNet; Conventional methods: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with hand-crafted features (Hist, LBP, HOG, Gabor, SIFT) and Bag of Visual Words (BoVW)
architecture_used.pretraining: CNN weights were pre-trained on ImageNet [10] and then fine-tuned. Non-pre-trained versions were also evaluated.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were downscaled to a specific size to fit the network (exact dimensions not reported) and normalized based on the mean and standard deviation of images in the ImageNet training set.

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Decayed by a factor of 10 every 10 epochs

classification_or_output_head.output: Binary label y ∈ {0, 1} indicating other symptoms of malignant tendency (positive) or subungual hematomas (negative)

preprocessing_techniques: Downscaling images to a specific size to fit the network architecture
Normalization based on the mean and standard deviation of images in the ImageNet training set
Data augmentation via random horizontal and vertical flipping
Removal of duplicate and similar images to maintain dataset diversity

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training data to augment the dataset. No specific probabilities or ranges were reported for the flipping operations.

conclusions: The authors introduced the Acral Melanoma Preliminary Screening Dataset (AMPSD), which is currently the largest dataset for acral melanoma, containing 6,066 dermoscopic images annotated by senior dermatologists.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) significantly outperform conventional hand-crafted feature-based methods (such as SIFT, LBP, HOG, Gabor, and Hist combined with SVM) in the task of automatic acral melanoma preliminary screening.

Conventional methods achieved poor performance (accuracy ranging from 0.61 to 0.71), which was attributed to the lack of consistency in texture, color, and shape, as well as the difficulty of object feature recognition for symptoms like bleeding under nails.
All tested CNNs achieved at least 0.90 accuracy and 0.95 AUROC, demonstrating their effectiveness for this task.
VGGNet achieved the best performance among the tested models with 0.9276 accuracy and 0.9666 AUROC.
More advanced CNNs like ResNet and DenseNet suffered from overfitting on this dataset, and increasing network depth did not yield significant performance gains.
Pre-trained networks (fine-tuned on ImageNet) performed better than non-pre-trained networks, with an accuracy improvement of approximately 3%.
The current performance of AI methods for acral melanoma screening still requires improvement due to data limitations and algorithm constraints.
The authors plan to maintain and expand the AMPSD dataset and develop methods that provide interpretability (reasons for prediction) to aid dermatologists and patients.
important_remarks: The dataset (AMPSD) was collected from a single hospital (Peking University First Hospital) over one year, limiting external validation and generalizability to other clinical settings.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance differences between models were not statistically validated.

Conventional hand-crafted feature methods performed poorly (accuracy 0.61-0.71), with the best result (SIFT) being only marginally better than random guessing due to the lack of consistency in texture, color, and shape of the symptoms.

Advanced CNNs (ResNet, DenseNet) suffered from overfitting on this dataset, indicating that increasing network depth did not yield performance gains and may be unsuitable for this specific data scale.

Pre-trained networks (fine-tuned on ImageNet) provided only a modest accuracy improvement of approximately 3% over non-pre-trained networks, suggesting limited benefit of transfer learning for this specific task.

The study addresses a binary classification task (subungual hematomas vs. malignant symptoms) rather than multi-class melanoma diagnosis, which limits its direct clinical applicability for differential diagnosis.
The dataset is imbalanced (4,116 malignant vs. 1,950 benign images), necessitating the use of AUROC alongside accuracy for evaluation, though class imbalance remains a potential bias source.
The paper acknowledges that current AI performance still requires improvement due to data limitations and algorithm constraints, and does not claim clinical readiness for deployment.
